title: How I used LLM to get concert lyrics | Hillel Wayne posted on the topic | LinkedIn
author: Hillel Wayne
text:
Vibe coding is great. A friend invited me to a concert later this week. Since I don't know the artist very well, I'd like to be able to follow along with the lyrics. And since internet reception at the venue is terrible, I'd like to get it all on my phone ahead of time. So here's how I vibed it: 1. Find the set playlist someone made on Spotify 2. Ask Claude 3.7 "Write a script that takes a hardcoded spotify playlist id, retrieves the lyrics for all of the songs in that playlist, and saves them to a text file." After a quick skim of the output script, I put in my playlist id and spotify credentials and it worked! My favorite bit? Spotify *doesn't have a lyrics API*. The script uses the API to find the song names, and then uses that to get the lyrics from Genius. Could I have done this on my own? Yeah, but it would have taken a couple of hours of figuring out the right Python packages and API calls, and that means I wouldn't have bothered at all. And odds are I'm never gonna touch either API again this year, so it's not like vibing cost me a learning opportunity. This is the biggest appeal of LLMs for me: doing easy drudgework. There's no mental challenge, no essential complexity, to piping output between bespoke APIs. Reading through Spotify response schemas doesn't make me a better programmer, it's just tedious and demotivating. Instead, I let Claude chug away in the background while I read a computational complexity paper, which was far more interesting and might actually be useful at some point in the future. There are some problems that are in my skillset as a programmer. There are a lot of problems that are still outside my developing skillset. And there's an enormous number of simple problems that are technically in my skillset but are so tedious I don't want to bother. Vibe coding makes me willing to bother, and that's amazing.
This is the right kind of vibe coding. A few months back I spent a day writing a script that, I later found out, would have taken 10 minutes to generate (and review). Lesson learnt!
Yes, but… You need to know when to use it, and when not to. You give a great example, simple, tedious, with no real consequences if it goes wrong. Would you sell the script as a product? Probably not. I’m sure you wouldn’t use it fly a plane. What about something like managing your savings accounts, or controlling your house alarm?
Hadn't heard of ` Vibe coding , ie: having AI flesh out the ` boiler-plate before . But in my SV-FIG presentation last Saturday ( linked at CoSy.com/CoSy/TekyDome.html#comment-6675401636 ) I questioned WHAT'S THE POINT OF A PROGRAMMING LANGUAGE IN THE AI FUTURE ? And a motivation for minimalist language is TO EXPRESS TASKS AS SUCCINCTLY AS YOU COULD ASK AI
